title: Discitis / Vertebral Osteomyelitis
oneLiner: Infection of disc and adjacent vertebral bodies; haematogenous spread (often from UTIs, skin, dental); can cause sepsis, epidural abscess, irreversible cord damage.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Spinal epidural abscess:** progressive motor weakness, loss of bladder/bowel control, or new saddle anaesthesia — needs emergency neuroimaging (MRI) and neurosurgery consult.
- **Sepsis / septic shock:** high fever + hypotension + altered mental status (especially in older, diabetic, or immunocompromised) – treat with IV fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics after cultures.
- **Cord compression from vertebral collapse:** acute onset severe back pain with bilateral leg weakness, hyperreflexia, upgoing plantars – urgent MRI and neurosurgical referral.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Localised back pain (thoracolumbar > cervical) – insidious onset, worse at night / supine, not relieved by rest
  - Fever / rigors (may be absent, especially in elderly)
  - Neurological deficits: radicular pain, leg weakness, numbness, sphincter dysfunction (late sign)
  - General malaise, anorexia, weight loss (if subacute)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Point spinal tenderness over affected level (single finger sign)
  - Paraspinal muscle spasm – resistance to forward flexion
  - Neurological exam: motor, sensory, reflexes; test perianal sensation and anal tone
  - Straight leg raise may be limited (if nerve root irritation)
  - Check for spine deformity / gibbus (advanced)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely with 1-line differentiators:**
  - Mechanical back pain (no fever, normal CRP, improves with movement)
  - Malignant spinal metastases (night pain, no fever, weight loss, worse at rest, normal CRP)
  - Inflammatory spondyloarthropathy (ankylosing spondylitis) – morning stiffness, improves with activity, young male, HLA-B27 positive
  - Psoas abscess (hip flexion deformity, fever, flank pain, may have no disc changes on MRI)
  - Pyelonephritis (loin pain, dysuria, positive urinalysis, no focal spinal tenderness)
  - **Splitter:** Elevated CRP/ESR + fever + focal spinal tenderness = infection (not malignancy or mechanical). If blood cultures negative but high suspicion, consider TB or brucellosis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Full neurological examination, including perianal sensation, anal tone, and bladder scan (post-void residual)
  - Vital signs – NEWS2, temperature, lactate if sepsis suspected
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR (elevated in bacterial; normal can’t rule out early)
  - Blood cultures x2 from separate sites (before antibiotics if possible)
  - Procalcitonin (if available) – more specific for bacterial infection than CRP
  - Consider HIV, hepatitis, TB testing if risk factors
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI whole spine with gadolinium (sensitivity >95% for discitis, epidural abscess, cord compression)
  - **Second-line:** CT with contrast (if MRI contraindicated – shows bone destruction, disc space narrowing, paraspinal collections)
  - **When to X-ray:** only if late presentation (works after >2 weeks – disc space narrowing, endplate erosion); do not delay MRI for X-ray
- **Special tests:**
  - Image-guided needle biopsy (if blood cultures negative and no response to empiric antibiotics) – send for culture, histology, TB PCR
  - Consider CT-guided aspiration for microbiological diagnosis
- **Never-miss test:** **MRI whole spine** – single most important investigation; missing an epidural abscess or cord compression is a serious error.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess and secure IV access; if septic: IV fluids (0.9% saline 500ml bolus, reassess)
  - Blood cultures x2, then start empiric IV antibiotics after discussion with microbiology (do not delay cultures if unstable)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol + NSAIDs (if no renal impairment or GI bleeding); avoid solely opiates – can mask neurological progression
  - Neurosurgery referral if any neurological deficit, epidural collection, or spinal instability
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Strict bed rest until spine stabilised or surgical opinion obtained (log-roll for turning, avoid twisting or bending)
  - Continue IV antibiotics – plan for long course (6-12 weeks total, starting with IV for at least 2 weeks)
  - Daily neurological examination (document motor, sensory, sphincter)
  - Consider spinal brace/orthosis if significant bony destruction (orthotics referral)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Switch to oral antibiotics after clinical and biochemical improvement (CRP trending down, afebrile, no new neurology) – guided by microbiology
  - Physiotherapy and graded mobilisation once spine stable (avoid heavy lifting, contact sports for 6 months)
  - Follow-up MRI to confirm resolution – usually at 6-12 weeks
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if no contraindication, antiplatelets not enough)
  - Observations: neuro obs (GCS, limb power) + vital signs 4 hourly; escalating if NEWS2 >=5
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 500ml over 8h if not on sepsis pathway, then oral fluids encouraged
  - Diet: may need high-protein/high-calorie if malnourished; NBM if surgery planned
  - NBM status: nil by mouth only if urgent MRI with contrast (non-urgent MRI can be done with food)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (empiric, until cultures):**
  - Flucloxacillin IV 2g QDS + Rifampicin PO 600mg BD (for sensitive Staph aureus) – **[check local policy]**
  - If penicillin allergy: Vancomycin IV (loading dose 25-30mg/kg, then 15-20mg/kg BD, adjust by trough levels) + Rifampicin – **[check local policy]**
  - If MRSA risk (IVDU, recent healthcare, colonisation): Vancomycin + Rifampicin +/- Teicoplanin – **[check local policy]**
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ceftriaxone IV 2g OD (for Gram-negative or community-acquired, if no Staph aureus cover needed) – **[check local policy]**
  - Clindamycin IV/PO (for bone penetration, if Gram-positive confirmed and no MRSA)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Rifampicin: strong CYP450 inducer – reduces efficacy of oral contraception, warfarin, antiepileptics, antiretrovirals. Inform patient and adjust other drugs.
  - Vancomycin: monitor trough levels (target 15-20mg/L), renal and auditory toxicity.
  - Flucloxacillin: increased risk of AKI with other nephrotoxics, cholestatic hepatitis (rare).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Vancomycin, gentamicin (if used) – adjust for renal impairment based on eGFR.
  - Rifampicin: generally safe in renal impairment, but no dose adjustment needed; hepatic impairment – caution.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting Vancomycin without checking baseline renal function and without ordering trough levels before second dose** – risk of nephrotoxicity and subtherapeutic levels.
  - **Not obtaining blood cultures before antibiotics** – lose diagnostic yield; if given, document reason and still obtain cultures before next dose if possible.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Spinal epidural abscess (can cause irreversible cord injury within hours)
  - Sepsis / septic shock (especially in elderly, diabetics, immunocompromised)
  - Pathological vertebral fracture / collapse causing spinal instability
  - Neuropathic pain / radiculopathy from nerve root compression
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic back pain, kyphosis, spinal deformity
  - Recurrent infection (if source not controlled or antibiotic course incomplete)
  - Neurological deficits (paraplegia, cauda equina syndrome)
  - Post-infective spondylotic changes (ankylosis)
sections.Prognosis:
- 80-90% cure rate with early diagnosis and appropriate prolonged antibiotics.
- Mortality <5% overall, but up to 20% if epidural abscess and delayed surgery.
- Neurological recovery: 60-70% if treated early; poor if complete paraplegia for >24 hours.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always obtain blood cultures (x2) before starting antibiotics** – if patient unstable, draw cultures and immediately start empiric abx, but still send.
- **Perform a full neurological exam including perianal sensation and anal tone** in every patient with back pain + fever or unexplained back pain.
- **Order a whole spine MRI with gadolinium** – discitis can be multifocal (up to 10%).
- **Consult neurosurgery or orthopaedic spine team early** if any neurological deficit or spinal instability.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t use NSAIDs as first-line analgesia without considering spine infection** – can mask fever and CRP response, delaying diagnosis.
- **Don’t start antibiotics blindly without cultures** unless septic shock – you lose microbiological diagnosis.
- **Don’t rely on X-ray to exclude discitis** – it can be normal for 2-4 weeks; MRI is essential.
- **Don’t forget to check for TB in atypical presentations** (subacute, night sweats, weight loss, negative bacterial cultures).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home – o:
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening back pain despite analgesia
  - Any leg weakness, numbness, or bladder/bowel loss of control (incontinence, retention)
  - Fever or rigors (especially during IV antibiotics)
  - Wound site (if biopsy) redness, discharge, or pain
- **Follow-up:**
  - Outpatient IV therapy team (if continuing IV abx at home)
  - Spine clinic or orthopaedic consultant within 2-4 weeks
  - Repeat MRI at 6-12 weeks (or sooner if clinical deterioration)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 >=5 or lactate >2 mmol/L (sepsis)
  - GCS drop or new confusion
  - New motor weakness, sensory level, or sphincter dysfunction (urgent neuroimaging + neurosurgery)
  - Haemodynamic instability unresponsive to fluid bolus (consider vasopressors and ICU)
  - Signs of spinal shock (hypotension, bradycardia, areflexia below lesion) – immediate neurosurgical emergency

rawMarkdown:
## Discitis / Vertebral Osteomyelitis
**One-liner:** Infection of disc and adjacent vertebral bodies; haematogenous spread (often from UTIs, skin, dental); can cause sepsis, epidural abscess, irreversible cord damage.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Spinal epidural abscess:** progressive motor weakness, loss of bladder/bowel control, or new saddle anaesthesia — needs emergency neuroimaging (MRI) and neurosurgery consult.
- **Sepsis / septic shock:** high fever + hypotension + altered mental status (especially in older, diabetic, or immunocompromised) – treat with IV fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics after cultures.
- **Cord compression from vertebral collapse:** acute onset severe back pain with bilateral leg weakness, hyperreflexia, upgoing plantars – urgent MRI and neurosurgical referral.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Localised back pain (thoracolumbar > cervical) – insidious onset, worse at night / supine, not relieved by rest
  - Fever / rigors (may be absent, especially in elderly)
  - Neurological deficits: radicular pain, leg weakness, numbness, sphincter dysfunction (late sign)
  - General malaise, anorexia, weight loss (if subacute)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Point spinal tenderness over affected level (single finger sign)
  - Paraspinal muscle spasm – resistance to forward flexion
  - Neurological exam: motor, sensory, reflexes; test perianal sensation and anal tone
  - Straight leg raise may be limited (if nerve root irritation)
  - Check for spine deformity / gibbus (advanced)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely with 1-line differentiators:**
  - Mechanical back pain (no fever, normal CRP, improves with movement)
  - Malignant spinal metastases (night pain, no fever, weight loss, worse at rest, normal CRP)
  - Inflammatory spondyloarthropathy (ankylosing spondylitis) – morning stiffness, improves with activity, young male, HLA-B27 positive
  - Psoas abscess (hip flexion deformity, fever, flank pain, may have no disc changes on MRI)
  - Pyelonephritis (loin pain, dysuria, positive urinalysis, no focal spinal tenderness)
  - **Splitter:** Elevated CRP/ESR + fever + focal spinal tenderness = infection (not malignancy or mechanical). If blood cultures negative but high suspicion, consider TB or brucellosis.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Full neurological examination, including perianal sensation, anal tone, and bladder scan (post-void residual)
  - Vital signs – NEWS2, temperature, lactate if sepsis suspected
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR (elevated in bacterial; normal can’t rule out early)
  - Blood cultures x2 from separate sites (before antibiotics if possible)
  - Procalcitonin (if available) – more specific for bacterial infection than CRP
  - Consider HIV, hepatitis, TB testing if risk factors
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI whole spine with gadolinium (sensitivity >95% for discitis, epidural abscess, cord compression)
  - **Second-line:** CT with contrast (if MRI contraindicated – shows bone destruction, disc space narrowing, paraspinal collections)
  - **When to X-ray:** only if late presentation (works after >2 weeks – disc space narrowing, endplate erosion); do not delay MRI for X-ray
- **Special tests:**
  - Image-guided needle biopsy (if blood cultures negative and no response to empiric antibiotics) – send for culture, histology, TB PCR
  - Consider CT-guided aspiration for microbiological diagnosis
- **Never-miss test:** **MRI whole spine** – single most important investigation; missing an epidural abscess or cord compression is a serious error.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess and secure IV access; if septic: IV fluids (0.9% saline 500ml bolus, reassess)
  - Blood cultures x2, then start empiric IV antibiotics after discussion with microbiology (do not delay cultures if unstable)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol + NSAIDs (if no renal impairment or GI bleeding); avoid solely opiates – can mask neurological progression
  - Neurosurgery referral if any neurological deficit, epidural collection, or spinal instability
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Strict bed rest until spine stabilised or surgical opinion obtained (log-roll for turning, avoid twisting or bending)
  - Continue IV antibiotics – plan for long course (6-12 weeks total, starting with IV for at least 2 weeks)
  - Daily neurological examination (document motor, sensory, sphincter)
  - Consider spinal brace/orthosis if significant bony destruction (orthotics referral)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Switch to oral antibiotics after clinical and biochemical improvement (CRP trending down, afebrile, no new neurology) – guided by microbiology
  - Physiotherapy and graded mobilisation once spine stable (avoid heavy lifting, contact sports for 6 months)
  - Follow-up MRI to confirm resolution – usually at 6-12 weeks
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if no contraindication, antiplatelets not enough)
  - Observations: neuro obs (GCS, limb power) + vital signs 4 hourly; escalating if NEWS2 >=5
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 500ml over 8h if not on sepsis pathway, then oral fluids encouraged
  - Diet: may need high-protein/high-calorie if malnourished; NBM if surgery planned
  - NBM status: nil by mouth only if urgent MRI with contrast (non-urgent MRI can be done with food)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (empiric, until cultures):**
  - Flucloxacillin IV 2g QDS + Rifampicin PO 600mg BD (for sensitive Staph aureus) – **[check local policy]**
  - If penicillin allergy: Vancomycin IV (loading dose 25-30mg/kg, then 15-20mg/kg BD, adjust by trough levels) + Rifampicin – **[check local policy]**
  - If MRSA risk (IVDU, recent healthcare, colonisation): Vancomycin + Rifampicin +/- Teicoplanin – **[check local policy]**
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ceftriaxone IV 2g OD (for Gram-negative or community-acquired, if no Staph aureus cover needed) – **[check local policy]**
  - Clindamycin IV/PO (for bone penetration, if Gram-positive confirmed and no MRSA)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Rifampicin: strong CYP450 inducer – reduces efficacy of oral contraception, warfarin, antiepileptics, antiretrovirals. Inform patient and adjust other drugs.
  - Vancomycin: monitor trough levels (target 15-20mg/L), renal and auditory toxicity.
  - Flucloxacillin: increased risk of AKI with other nephrotoxics, cholestatic hepatitis (rare).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Vancomycin, gentamicin (if used) – adjust for renal impairment based on eGFR.
  - Rifampicin: generally safe in renal impairment, but no dose adjustment needed; hepatic impairment – caution.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting Vancomycin without checking baseline renal function and without ordering trough levels before second dose** – risk of nephrotoxicity and subtherapeutic levels.
  - **Not obtaining blood cultures before antibiotics** – lose diagnostic yield; if given, document reason and still obtain cultures before next dose if possible.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Spinal epidural abscess (can cause irreversible cord injury within hours)
  - Sepsis / septic shock (especially in elderly, diabetics, immunocompromised)
  - Pathological vertebral fracture / collapse causing spinal instability
  - Neuropathic pain / radiculopathy from nerve root compression
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic back pain, kyphosis, spinal deformity
  - Recurrent infection (if source not controlled or antibiotic course incomplete)
  - Neurological deficits (paraplegia, cauda equina syndrome)
  - Post-infective spondylotic changes (ankylosis)

### Prognosis
- 80-90% cure rate with early diagnosis and appropriate prolonged antibiotics.
- Mortality <5% overall, but up to 20% if epidural abscess and delayed surgery.
- Neurological recovery: 60-70% if treated early; poor if complete paraplegia for >24 hours.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always obtain blood cultures (x2) before starting antibiotics** – if patient unstable, draw cultures and immediately start empiric abx, but still send.
- **Perform a full neurological exam including perianal sensation and anal tone** in every patient with back pain + fever or unexplained back pain.
- **Order a whole spine MRI with gadolinium** – discitis can be multifocal (up to 10%).
- **Consult neurosurgery or orthopaedic spine team early** if any neurological deficit or spinal instability.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t use NSAIDs as first-line analgesia without considering spine infection** – can mask fever and CRP response, delaying diagnosis.
- **Don’t start antibiotics blindly without cultures** unless septic shock – you lose microbiological diagnosis.
- **Don’t rely on X-ray to exclude discitis** – it can be normal for 2-4 weeks; MRI is essential.
- **Don’t forget to check for TB in atypical presentations** (subacute, night sweats, weight loss, negative bacterial cultures).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home – only after MRI and antibiotics established, neurologically stable)
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening back pain despite analgesia
  - Any leg weakness, numbness, or bladder/bowel loss of control (incontinence, retention)
  - Fever or rigors (especially during IV antibiotics)
  - Wound site (if biopsy) redness, discharge, or pain
- **Follow-up:**
  - Outpatient IV therapy team (if continuing IV abx at home)
  - Spine clinic or orthopaedic consultant within 2-4 weeks
  - Repeat MRI at 6-12 weeks (or sooner if clinical deterioration)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 >=5 or lactate >2 mmol/L (sepsis)
  - GCS drop or new confusion
  - New motor weakness, sensory level, or sphincter dysfunction (urgent neuroimaging + neurosurgery)
  - Haemodynamic instability unresponsive to fluid bolus (consider vasopressors and ICU)
  - Signs of spinal shock (hypotension, bradycardia, areflexia below lesion) – immediate neurosurgical emergency
